commands.name: Key-Value Pairs (Mappings)
commands.description: The fundamental building block of YAML, mapping an identifier (key) to a value. Must be separated by a colon and a space.
commands.example:
project_name: OpenDev Hub
version: 1.2.0
status: active
commands.mistake: Omitting the space after the colon (e.g. 'key:value'). Parsers will treat this as a single string instead of a key-value mapping.

commands.name: Comments
commands.description: Inline documentation or notes. The YAML parser ignores anything starting with a hash symbol.
commands.example:
port: 8080 # Run service on port 8080
# Database credentials below:
commands.mistake: Attempting multi-line block comments using /* ... */ or <!-- ... -->. YAML only supports single-line comments starting with '#'.

commands.name: Document Separator (Start)
commands.description: Marks the beginning of a new YAML document within a single file or stream, allowing multi-document configurations.
commands.example:
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-pod
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-service
commands.mistake: Placing configurations above the first '---' block, which can cause inconsistent behavior across different parsers.

commands.name: Document Separator (End)
commands.description: Explicitly marks the end of a YAML document. It is optional but useful for streaming protocols to signal completion.
commands.example:
---
message: Hello World
...
# End of document, further text starts a new session
commands.mistake: Confusing '...' with '---'. Use '---' to split documents and '...' to mark the complete termination of the document stream.

commands.name: Indentation & Hierarchy
commands.description: YAML uses whitespace indentation to define structure and parent-child relationships instead of brackets or braces.
commands.mistake: Using tab characters for indentation. YAML strictly forbids tabs. Ensure your editor converts tab keys to spaces.

commands.name: Integers
commands.description: Represents whole numbers. Supports decimal, hexadecimal (base 16), octal (base 8), and binary (base 2).
commands.mistake: Writing octal values as '0755' instead of '0o755'. In YAML 1.2, leading zeros without 'o' are parsed as decimals (755), not octals.

commands.name: Floating Point Numbers
commands.description: Represents real numbers with fractional parts, exponential notation, or special values like infinity and NaN.
commands.mistake: Omitting decimals in exponential values or assuming standard JSON parsers will handle '.inf' and '.nan' values without preprocessing.

commands.name: Booleans
commands.description: Represents logical truth values (true or false).
commands.mistake: In YAML 1.1, values like 'yes/no', 'on/off' are treated as booleans. If you write 'country: NO' (for Norway), it will be parsed as 'false'. Use quotes: 'country: "NO"'.

commands.name: Nulls
commands.description: Represents missing, undefined, or empty values.
commands.mistake: Assuming an empty quoted string 'key: ""' is parsed as null. It is parsed as a string of length zero. Use unquoted 'null' or '~' for nulls.

commands.name: Timestamps & Dates
commands.description: Represents calendar dates and times using ISO 8601 formatting.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to quote timestamps when they need to be treated as plain strings by the application (e.g. database keys or version labels).

commands.name: Unquoted (Plain) Strings
commands.description: Strings written without single or double quotes. Convenient but subject to parsing restrictions.
commands.example: greeting: Hello and welcome to OpenDev Hub
commands.mistake: Including special YAML characters like ':', '#', '{', '}', '[', ']', or '&' in plain strings. If a string starts with or contains these characters, it must be quoted.

commands.name: Single-Quoted Strings
commands.description: Strings enclosed in single quotes. Preserves all characters as literals and does not process escape sequences.
commands.mistake: Attempting to escape characters with backslashes (e.g., '\n' inside single quotes renders as a literal backslash followed by 'n'). To escape a single quote itself, double it: 'It''s working'.

commands.name: Double-Quoted Strings
commands.description: Strings enclosed in double quotes. Processes escape sequences like '\n' (newline), '\t' (tab), and unicode characters.
commands.example: welcome_message: "Welcome to OpenDev Hub!\nEnjoy your stay.\t🚀"
commands.mistake: Forgetting to escape backslashes when they should be literal (e.g., 'path: "C:\temp"' will escape '\t' as a tab). Use double backslashes: 'path: "C:\\temp"'.

commands.name: Literal Block Scalar (|)
commands.description: Preserves all newlines and trailing whitespace in a multi-line string. Ideal for code blocks, logs, or certificates.
commands.syntax:
key: |
  indented multiline string
  second line
commands.mistake: Using a tab for indentation within the block or indenting the block lines less than or equal to the parent key's indentation.

commands.name: Folded Block Scalar (>)
commands.description: Replaces single newlines in a multi-line block with spaces, folding the text into a single paragraph. Double newlines (blank lines) are preserved.
commands.example:
meta_description: >
  OpenDev Hub is a neobrutalist platform
  built for modern developers.
  It provides fast tools, cheatsheets,
  and live event trackers.
commands.mistake: Expecting newlines to be preserved. If line breaks are important (such as for scripting or certs), use the Literal scalar '|' instead.

commands.name: Chomping Indicators (+ / -)
commands.description: Appended to '|' or '>' block scalars to control trailing newlines. Strip (-) removes all trailing newlines, Keep (+) retains them, and default Clip keeps exactly one.
commands.example:
private_key: |-
  -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
  MIIEvgIBADANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAASCBKgwggSkAgEAAoIBAQC3
  -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
license_footer: |+
  Copyright (c) 2026 OpenDev Hub
commands.mistake: Omitting the chomping indicator on files that are sensitive to trailing whitespace, such as private keys, breaking SSH handshakes.

commands.name: Block Sequences (Lists)
commands.description: Ordered lists of values represented by bullet points using hyphens, aligned at the same indentation level.
commands.mistake: Forgetting the space after the hyphen (e.g. '-item'). This will parse as a single plain string '-item' rather than a list element.

commands.name: Flow Sequences (Inline Lists)
commands.description: Inline lists wrapped in square brackets, similar to JSON array syntax.
commands.mistake: Omitting commas between list elements or mixing block sequences with flow sequences without proper indentation.

commands.name: Block Mappings (Dictionaries)
commands.description: Key-value pairs organized on multiple lines using indentation.
commands.mistake: Keys in the same mapping aligned at different indentation levels, which shifts them into nested or separate parents.

commands.name: Flow Mappings (Inline Dictionaries)
commands.description: Inline key-value pairs wrapped in curly braces, matching JSON object syntax.
commands.example: headers: { Content-Type: application/json, Authorization: Bearer token123 }
commands.mistake: Omitting the space after the colon inside the curly braces. Unlike JSON, YAML flow mappings require '{key: value}' with space.

commands.name: Nested Collections
commands.description: Combining mappings and sequences to build complex, multi-dimensional structures.
commands.example:
environments:
  - name: production
    servers:
      - ip: 192.168.1.10
        role: web
      - ip: 192.168.1.11
        role: db
  - name: staging
    servers:
      - ip: 192.168.2.10
        role: all
commands.mistake: Improperly aligning hyphens with keys. Subkeys of a list item must be indented consistently with the start of the item.

commands.name: Anchors (&)
commands.description: Marks a block of YAML data with a reference name, allowing it to be reused or duplicated later in the file.
commands.mistake: Attempting to reference an anchor that has not been defined yet. Anchors must be declared higher up in the file before they are used.

commands.name: Aliases (*)
commands.description: References a defined anchor to duplicate its content inline without re-writing it.
commands.mistake: Using the alias prefix '*' inside double quotes (e.g. '*defaults'). This will make the parser read it as a literal string '*defaults' instead of resolving the alias.

commands.name: Merge Keys (<<)
commands.description: Used in combination with anchors and aliases to inject all key-values of a referenced map into the current map, allowing overrides.
commands.example:
production_env:
  <<: *defaults
  timeout: 10 # Overrides the default 30
  debug: false
commands.mistake: Using merge keys (<<) in YAML 1.2 environments where the parser strictly adheres to 1.2 rules (as << was a YAML 1.1 type). Most modern parsers still support it for compatibility, but check parser docs.

commands.name: Explicit Tags (Data Type Casts)
commands.description: Overrides YAML's implicit type detection by explicitly prefixing values with a type tag.
commands.mistake: Typing tags incorrectly (e.g., single exclamation mark '!str' instead of double exclamation marks '!!str'). Custom tags use '!', standard built-ins use '!!'.

commands.name: Sets (!!set)
commands.description: A collection of unique items where order is not guaranteed. Indicated by the !!set tag, where keys are separated by a question mark.
commands.mistake: Adding duplicate items to a set. The set will collapse duplicates during parsing, resulting in only unique entries.

commands.name: Ordered Mappings (!!omap)
commands.description: A sequence of key-value pairs where key order is strictly preserved, unlike standard maps which are unordered.
commands.example:
deployment_steps: !!omap
  - checkout: git clone
  - install: npm install
  - build: npm run build
  - deploy: pm2 restart app
commands.mistake: Formatting ordered mappings as a map rather than a sequence of single-key maps (e.g. using bullet points with hyphens). Each key-value pair in an omap must be a sequence element.

commands.name: Binary Data (!!binary)
commands.description: Encodes binary files (like images, zip files, or PDFs) directly in YAML as a Base64-encoded string.
commands.mistake: Leaving out the literal block scalar '|'. Binary data must be formatted as a block scalar to support line folding and spaces in the base64 string.
